html:
1. Dataset Characteristics

  • Rating Distribution: moderately imbalanced (Rating 5★ leads at 44.19%.)
  • Review Length: Overall average of 104.38 words and average of 724.90 characters per review (raw, all words included). Negative reviews are notably longer with average 126.60 words (867 characters) for 2★ reviews, and 5★ reviews at only 93.96 words (661 characters). Frustrated guests write more; satisfied guests write less.
  • Corpus Size: 2,138,765 total raw words across all 20,491 reviews
  • Stop Words Density: Only 45,273 stop words detected (2.1% of corpus), which far below typical English prose (30–40%).
  • Vocabulary Size: 102,008 tokens before cleaning → 75,048 after removing stop words & non-alpha (26.4% reduction)
  • Data Quality: 0 missing values, 0 duplicate rows, which is an exceptionally clean dataset

2. Vocabulary & Language Patterns

  • Vocabulary Richness by Rating (after cleaning): 5★ has ~5 times more total words than 1★ (reflects class imbalance), and also ~3 times more unique vocabulary, i.e., positive reviews have richer, more varied language

3. Data Processing

  • Raw Stats: Word/character counts include ALL words (not, no, nothing, none, cant, etc.)
  • Vocabulary Stats: Word frequency, TF-IDF, and bigram analyses computed AFTER removing stop words, punctuation, numbers
  • Stop Word Anomaly: 2.1% stop word rate (vs typical 30–40%) suggests text is already preprocessed or written in a telegraphic/informal style common to review platforms — affects absolute stop word counts but not relative vocabulary analysis
  • Why TF-IDF + Bigrams: Top raw frequencies (hotel, room) are identical across all ratings, which is meaningless for discrimination. TF-IDF surfaces weighted terms (nt, staff) while Bigrams restore context ("credit card" vs "great location")
